facts_disclosed: Second shop plan is dead — Burak gone, rent too high

turns.content: can I ask you something... about business. I am thinking a lot these days, maybe too much
Of course, go ahead. What's on your mind?
you know... when you have a plan for a long time, and you believe in it, really believe... and then one day you look at it and you think, this is not going to happen. how do you let go of that

That's a really honest question, and it's one a lot of business owners struggle with. Letting go of a plan you believed in doesn't mean you failed — sometimes it means the conditions changed or you grew past it. Can you tell me more about what the plan was? That might help me give you something more specific.

I had this idea... a second shop. in Moda, you know, the nice part of Kadıköy. I was going to have my apprentice run it, I trained him for years... good location, good foot traffic. I was saving money, looking at spaces. I really thought this was the next chapter

That sounds like it was a significant dream — expanding your craft to a second location with someone you trained. What happened? Did the circumstances change, or did something specific make you reconsider?

everything changed. my apprentice... Burak, he left. opened his own place in another neighbourhood. I cannot blame him, that is the way it should be, the student becomes the master... but without him there is nobody to run a second shop. and the rent in Moda now... wallahi, it is insane. they want numbers that make no sense for a barbershop

Ah, that's a double blow — losing the person who would have run it AND the rent becoming unrealistic. Either one of those alone could derail an expansion plan, but both together... that changes the equation completely. How much has the rent gone up in that area?

when I first looked maybe two years ago... it was high but possible. now they are asking double. all these fancy cafes and boutiques moved in, the landlords think every shop is a goldmine. but a barbershop is not a café selling 80 lira lattes... we work with our hands, one customer at a time. the margins are different

You're absolutely right — the economics of a skilled trade shop are completely different from a trendy café. Your revenue ceiling is limited by how many chairs you have and how many hours you work. When commercial rent inflates because of gentrification, it pushes out exactly the kinds of businesses that give a neighbourhood its character.

So where does that leave you with the plan?

the Moda shop... forget about it. that dream is dead. Burak is gone, the rent is crazy, and honestly... I am 56 years old. one shop is enough. I have been telling myself this for weeks now and it is time to say it out loud. it is off the table, completely

There's actually something really powerful about saying it out loud. You're not giving up — you're making a clear-eyed decision based on what's real right now. A second shop without the right person to run it, at unsustainable rent, at this stage of your career — that's not a dream worth chasing anymore. It's a trap.

What does that free up for you? Sometimes when you let go of one plan, energy opens up for something else.

you know... we have a saying in Turkish. 'iki tavşanı kovalayan ikisini de kaçırır'... he who chases two rabbits catches neither. I would rather make my shop the best it can be than spread myself thin. I have a new apprentice now, 17 years old, starting from zero... he cannot even lather properly yet. maybe my energy goes there. make him good. make the shop better. this is enough

That's a great proverb and it fits perfectly here. Focusing your energy on one excellent shop and mentoring a new apprentice — that's not settling, that's clarity. Training the next generation of your craft is its own kind of expansion, just not the kind that requires a lease.

How's it going with the new apprentice so far?

Deniz... he is willing, I will say that. but willing and skilled are two different things. with Burak I had 3 years of training already done... now I start again from the beginning. the lather, the comb, the posture, everything. but you know what... there is something nice about it too. teaching is... it gives you purpose. Hasan usta, my old master, he used to say 'the best barber is the one who makes a better barber.' I understand that now

That's a beautiful way to think about it. Your master's words clearly stuck with you, and now you're living them out with Deniz. It's a different kind of legacy than a second shop — maybe even a more meaningful one.

It sounds like you've actually already made peace with this decision, even if it took some time to say it out loud.

ja... yes. I think I have. it was hard, you know. I spent maybe a year saving, planning, imagining the sign on the door... 'Mehmet Usta - Moda.' but some things are not meant to be. and that is okay. the shop I have... it is my second home. 30 years. why would I need two homes when I cannot even keep one back from hurting, hah... my back is killing me today, I stood 9 hours already

Ha — that's a very practical argument against expansion too. If nine hours on your feet in one shop already has your back in trouble, imagine splitting yourself between two locations.

You should sit down and rest. Seriously. You've made a good decision today, and your shop isn't going anywhere. Take care of that back.

you are right... Ayşe says the same thing. 'sit down Mehmet, you are not 30 anymore.' okay my friend. thank you. it helped to talk about this. sometimes you carry a thing in your head for weeks and you just need to... let it go. the Moda dream is finished and I am at peace with it. güle güle

Güle güle, Mehmet. You made a wise choice, and saying it out loud was the right move. One great shop, one new apprentice, and a craft you've spent a lifetime perfecting — that's more than enough. Rest that back tonight.
